canonical_definition: The term PRISM is used in the provided literature to refer to two distinct and unrelated entities. One paper presents PRISM as a 'globally diverse preference dataset' with a specific focus on 'values and controversial opinions' ("Uncertainty in Causality: A New Frontier"). This dataset is described as being composed of multi-turn conversations between users and various LLMs, where users provide N-way preference ratings. In contrast, another study uses PRISM as an acronym for 'Posterior-Refined Inference for Subset Membership,' which is defined as a 'data-blind attack method for fragment inference' ("Fragments to Facts: Partial-Information Fragment Inference from LLMs"). This method is designed to reduce false positives in attacks by using a likelihood ratio score that incorporates a prior probability from an external 'world model'. The provided data does not suggest any connection between the dataset and the attack method, indicating a homonym within the research landscape.
papers: Evaluating Multimodal Language Models as Visual Assistants for Visually Impaired Users
Uncertainty in Causality: A New Frontier
Fragments to Facts: Partial-Information Fragment Inference from LLMs
Position: Challenges and Future Directions of Data-Centric AI Alignment
Position: Trustworthy AI Agents Require the Integration of Large Language Models and Formal Methods

definitions.text: A globally diverse preference dataset focusing on values and controversial opinions, where users engage in multi-turn conversations with LLMs and provide N-way preference ratings.
definitions.paper: Uncertainty in Causality: A New Frontier
